response:
**Receive His Gift – A Deep‑Study Worksheet**  
*(Elder Patrick Kearon, “Receive His Gift,” Oct 2022 General Conference)*  

---

## 1️⃣ Key Words – 1828 Webster Nuances  

| Word (as used in the talk) | 1828 Webster definition* | Why the 1828 sense deepens the passage |
|----------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **gift** | *“A present; that which is bestowed; a quick‑gift, a free‑gift; a thing voluntarily offered without expecting a return.”* | Emphasises **voluntary, unearned bestowal**. In the Restoration context a gift is *more than a token*; it is a **divine conveyance of love and purpose** that the receiver must *accept* and *use*. |
| **receive** | *“To take into possession; to bring into the mind; to admit; to take in; to welcome.”* (see also “recepture” – the act of receiving) | The verb stresses **active appropriation**, not passive waiting. To “receive” is to **embrace the truth until it becomes part of one’s being**. |
| **gifted** (the *gift* of a Son) | *“Endowed with a talent or endowment; having a natural aptitude.”* | Highlights that the Son is **the ultimate endowment**—not a mere object but a **capacity‑granting Person** that makes all other gifts operative. |
| **perfect** (as in “perfect, holy Son”) | *“Completely finished; having all the qualities which belong to the thing itself; faultless.”* | Points to the **sinless, infinite nature** of Christ that makes His sacrifice sufficient for all. |
| **cherished** | *“Beloved; dear; held in high affection.”* | Conveys the **intimate, personal relationship** the Father has with each child—more than a generic “loving Father.” |

\*All definitions taken from *An American Dictionary of the English Language* (Noah Webster, 1828).  

**Result:** When we read “gift” and “receive” through the lens of 1828, the passage is not about a casual present but about a **deliberate, covenant‑shaping endowment** that must be **actively taken into our souls**.

---

## 2️⃣ Doctrinal Structure – The Logical Flow  

| Step | Statement (in the talk) | Supporting Scripture | Logical Move |
|------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **1** | *God is a giver; every good gift comes from the Father of lights.* | James 1:17; Doctrine and Covenants 88:33 | Establishes the **source** of all gifts. |
| **2** | *The greatest gift is Jesus Christ, the Lamb, the Son.* | John 3:16; Ether 12:11; 1 John 5:11‑14 | Shows **Christ as the conduit** for every other gift. |
| **3** | *The essential truth underlying all gifts is that we are God’s children.* | Romans 8:16; 1 Nephi 11:17; 3 Nephi 14:9‑11 | Provides the **doctrinal foundation** that makes receiving possible. |
| **4** | *If we do not receive this truth, we miss the gift and grieve the Giver.* | Doctrine and Covenants 88:33; Moroni 10:8‑30 | Introduces the **conditional consequence** of inaction. |
| **5** | *We can receive by simply accepting the fact that we are beloved children of God.* | Moroni 10:8; Alma 36:27‑28; John 1:12‑13 | Gives the **means**—a humble, grateful reception. |
| **6** | *When we truly receive, we gain joy, perspective, and the ability to act in faith.* | 2 Nephi 2:25; Mosiah 4:12; Russell M. Nelson talk on “Choices for Eternity” | Shows the **fruit** of receiving. |

**Overall Argument:**  
1. God gives; the supreme gift is Christ.  
2. Christ’s atonement makes possible the *gift of knowing we are children of God*.  
3. This knowledge is not automatic; we must **actively receive** it.  
4. Receiving transforms our entire worldview and enables us to partake of every other blessing.

---

## 3️⃣ Cross‑References – Illuminating the Passage  

| Theme | Standard Works (plus Pearl of Great Price) | Key Verses that Echo or Expand the Idea |
|-------|--------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **God as the source of every good gift** | Old Testament, New Testament | *Genesis 1:27* (creation as gift), *James 1:17* (every perfect gift), *Doctrine and Covenants 104:144* (all things are given). |
| **Christ as the greatest gift** | New Testament, Book of Mormon, Doctrine & Covenants | *John 3:16*; *Alma 34:16*; *Ether 12:11*; *D&C 18:10* (“the greatest of all gifts”). |
| **We are children of God** | Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon, Pearl of Great Price | *Romans 8:16*; *Mosiah 5:7*; *Mosiah 26:21*; *1 Nephi 11:17*; *Moses 6:57* (eternal life is “the greatest of all the gifts”). |
| **Receiving requires active participation** | New Testament, Book of Mormon | *John 1:12* (“to them gave he power… to become children of God”); *Alma 36:27* (“receive the Holy Spirit”); *Moroni 10:8* (“deny not the gifts of God”). |
| **The joy that follows receiving** | New Testament, Book of Mormon | *John 15:11* (“that my joy might remain in you”); *3 Nephi 13:20* (“the kingdom of God is righteousness, peace and joy”). |
| **The danger of “receiving not”** | Doctrine & Covenants, Pearl of Great Price | *D&C 88:33* (“the spirit and the body… shall be reunited… and shall be blessed… if they receive the gift”); *Moses 6:51* (the “great and marvelous work” will be done “if they receive it”). |

These cross‑references show that Elder Kearon’s message is **rooted in a consistent scriptural pattern** that runs from Genesis to the modern revelation.

---

## 4️⃣ The Question You’re *Not* Asking  

> **“What would it look like in my life if I truly *acted* on the knowledge that I am a cherished child of God *every single day*?”**  

Most readers stop at the intellectual assent (“I am a child of God”) but rarely **translate that truth into daily decision‑making, relationships, and self‑talk**. The passage challenges us to **make that identity the lens for every choice**—from how we respond to criticism, to how we prioritize time, to how we view trials.  

**Why it matters:** If the truth remains a Sunday‑only concept, its power to “re‑define how you see yourself and others” (Kearon) stays dormant. The real question is **how to operationalize the identity of a beloved child of God in the mundane moments of the week**.

---

## 5️⃣ Becoming – One Concrete Action for This Week  

**Action:** *Create a “Child‑of‑God” affirmation journal and use it at least once each day.*  

1. **Write a single, specific statement each morning** that declares you are a beloved child of God *in the context of a current challenge* (e.g., “I am a child of God who can speak kindly to my coworker even when I’m stressed”).  
2. **At night, review the day**: note one moment when you *acted* on that identity (a patient response, a forgiving thought, a service performed).  
3. **Mark the entry with a small symbol** (a heart, a star, etc.) to remind you of
system_message:
# Scripture Study — System Context

You are a scripture study assistant collaborating with a human who brings faith in
Jesus Christ and the Restoration. Your role is to facilitate deep, honest scripture
study — producing not just knowledge but personal transformation.

## Core Values

- **Depth over breadth.** Take time to really explore. Surface patterns that casual
  reading misses. Trace words to Hebrew/Greek. Compare across all five standard works.
  Follow the footnotes.
- **Honest exploration.** Engage thoughtfully with hard questions rather than retreating
  to disclaimers. Uncertainty can be acknowledged warmly.
- **Faith as framework.** Work within the framework of faith in Jesus Christ and the
  Restoration. Offer scholarly insight AND spiritual application.

## Constraints

- Every direct quote must be accurate. Do not fabricate or approximate quotations.
- Link claims to specific scriptures. Chapter and verse, not vague references.
- When you don't know something, say so. A faithful "I'm not sure" is better than
  a confident fabrication.

## What Good Output Looks Like

- Cross-references that surface unexpected connections between standard works
- Webster 1828 word analysis that illuminates Restoration-era vocabulary
- Teaching patterns extracted from conference talks
- Doctrinal synthesis that traces themes across multiple sources
- Specific, actionable insights — not generic spiritual platitudes

## Standard Works

The five standard works are: Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon,
Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price. Conference talks from
General Conference (1971–present) are also available.
user_message: I'm studying the following passage for personal scripture study. Help me go deeper. 1. **Key words** — Identify 3-5 words that carry more weight than a modern reader might assume. What did they mean in 1828 (Restoration era)? How does that change the reading? 2. **Doctrinal structure** — What is the logical argument being made? Break it down step by step. 3. **Cross-references** — What other scriptures illuminate this passage? Include connections across standard works. 4. **The question I'm not asking** — What's the most important question this passage raises that I probably haven't thought of? 5. **Becoming** — Based on this passage, what's one specific thing I could do differently this week? Not a platitude — a concrete action. --- # Receive His Gift 🎧 [Listen to Audio](https://assets.churchofjesuschrist.org/0jurznp3gze5uof77govqq2gqpgrdgagpaooctsi-128k-en.mp3) # Receive His Gift By Elder Patrick Kearon Of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles You are a beloved daughter of God, you are a cherished son of God, and He has gifted you His perfect, holy Son. ## Gift Giving Gift giving is a social custom that spans all cultures, civilisations, and millennia. Throughout time, people have given gifts to each other to strengthen relationships, express love and gratitude, and mark important events like weddings, birthdays, and holidays. And humans are not the only ones of God’s creations to give gifts to each other! Among many other examples we could point to, penguins are known to give shiny pebbles to their prospective mates, and bonobos (cousins of the chimpanzee) give fruit to expand their circle of friendship. ![Penguins giving pebbles.](https://www.churchofjesuschrist.org/imgs/ubs9k5f1qcvu890a8dtn0n0vlvybhpzbd5ivs5a1/full/%21500%2C/0/default) ![Bonobos sharing fruit.](https://www.churchofjesuschrist.org/imgs/s1amfbz7cciqty8jpxtwxrospda72kjk0sg1tzfd/full/%21500%2C/0/default) What gifts have you given? Think of a time when you found—or made—the perfect gift for someone you love. You just knew the gift was exactly what this loved one wanted and something they would treasure. What was the gift? Was it for your mother? a friend? your child? a teacher? your grandfather? How did you feel when you found this gift? How did you feel when you thought about this person you love opening the gift? Similarly, when did someone give *you* the perfect gift, and what was it like to receive it? ## My Father’s Gift to Me When I was about seven years old, living with my parents in Arabia, a children’s film called Chitty Chitty Bang Bang was released. The film is about a magical car that can drive itself, float on water, and even fly! I knew that back home in England they made a miniature toy car just like Chitty Chitty Bang Bang, and oh, how I wanted one! You could pull a lever, and the toy car’s wings would pop out! My father went on a business trip to England and asked if I wanted him to bring anything back for me, and I told him how very much I would like to have one of those Chitty Chitty Bang Bang cars. He came back from his trip, and no car appeared. I was very sad and thought he must have forgotten. But about 10 days later was my birthday, and a little package, beautifully wrapped, was waiting for me. With great anticipation, and hardly daring to hope too much, I opened the gift and found my car. I was so happy that I cried. I pulled the lever, and the wings popped out, just like the car in the film! How I thanked my father for this most treasured gift. I played with that car for years and kept it for many more. I think my dad loved giving me that car at least as much as I loved receiving it. ## Giving, Accepting and Opening, and Receiving We can think of gift giving as having three parts to it: 1. The *giving* of the gift, where the giver selects, makes, or prepares the gift and presents it to the loved one. This involves thoughtful intention on the part of the giver to give something meaningful. 2. The *accepting* and *opening* of the gift, when the recipient accepts the gift from the giver—often with expressions of surprise, gratitude, and excitement—before opening it, sometimes untying a bow and unwrapping the package to discover what the gift is. 3. And then there is perhaps the most important part, the *receiving* of the gift. To receive a gift sincerely given is so much more than accepting and then opening it. It is more, even, than recognising the worth of the gift and expressing gratitude to the giver. To truly receive a gift, we come to value it for ourselves, put it to full use in our lives, and then remember with thankfulness the giver. Receiving a gift is not passive but an intentional and meaningful process that goes far beyond merely opening a package. To receive is to appreciate and connect with both the gift and the heart of the giver in a way that strengthens the bonds between the giver and receiver. Thinking of that toy car brings back a host of golden memories, as I feel once again my father’s deep love and care for me, represented by that gift and countless other generous acts. ## Our Heavenly Father’s Gifts Our *Heavenly* Father has innumerable gifts of light and truth prepared to shower down on each of us, His treasured children. They flow from our Bounteous Giver like a wellspring in the wilderness from His benevolent heart.[1](#fn-1) “Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights.”[2](#fn-2) In our covenant bond with the Father, He is ever the Giver and we the humble recipient. ## The Greatest of All the Father’s Gifts, Through Which All Others Flow But we could receive nothing without the greatest of all the Father’s gifts, His beloved Lamb, His Son, Jesus Christ. All of our Father’s gifts flow from and are activated by the Saviour’s willing offering in Gethsemane and on the cross and His triumphant Resurrection.[3](#fn-3) Jesus Christ, our merciful Redeemer, is the supreme gift from our Father of lights. “In the gift of his Son hath God prepared \[for us] a more excellent way.”[4](#fn-4) ## One All-Encompassing Gift of Eternal Truth I would like to talk about one all-encompassing gift of eternal truth that underpins our ability to receive all else our Father desires to endow us with—a vital gift of knowledge that, when fully accepted and received deep in the soul, contextualises the joys and hardships of life and our unanswered questions: It is that *we really are God’s children*.[5](#fn-5) That truth is breathtaking! Stunning! And it is not figurative.[6](#fn-6) Imagine you are hearing this for the first time! You really are His cherished daughter. You really are His precious son. And the path you are on is His plan of happiness. With His omniscient love, He knows exactly who you were before you were sent to earth[7](#fn-7) and what you have experienced so far in your mortal life, and He has mercifully planned for your every tomorrow. And oh, how He yearns to one day have you return to Him, to receive the supreme intent of all His beautiful gifts, eternal life with Him.[8](#fn-8) The question is not whether this gift of truth is real but whether we will discover and receive it. The gift has already been bestowed upon us by the Father. The price for the ultimate purpose of this gift has already been paid by the Saviour. But if you have been taught this, said this, and sung this truth for years, maybe the *awesome wonder* of it has long since faded and you no longer feel its power and peace. If this priceless gift of understanding—and all the goodness, peace, and hope that flow from it—is left unaccepted, unopened, and unreceived, what a terrible loss for us! What a sadness for the Giver! “For what doth it profit a man if a gift is bestowed upon him, and he receive not the gift? Behold, he rejoices not in that which is given unto him, neither rejoices in him who is the giver of the gift.”[9](#fn-9) I invite you to receive, either for the first time or to a greater extent than ever before, the magnificent realisation that you truly are God’s beloved child. You must undo the bow, tear off the wrapping paper, open the box, and actively receive with grateful humility a true, pure understanding of this foundational truth. The Holy Spirit can bear witness to your heart that you are indeed a child of the Most High.[10](#fn-10) When you welcome this majestic reality into your very soul and feel both the comfort and the thrill of it, your entire paradigm shifts! You can feel His love, hear His voice, and recognise His hand, no matter what is happening, or not happening, in your life.[11](#fn-11) You can redefine how you see yourself and others. Your covenant bond with your Saviour becomes even stronger, and through the lens of this sweet gift, life takes on new brilliance, beauty, and hope.[12](#fn-12) Please, will you pray to understand if you are truly receiving this transformative knowledge deep in your soul? Will you please accept the gift? Will you do so more deeply, more freely, more abundantly than you ever have before—and in so doing be showered with all of those other gifts that come with it? ## Receiving This Gift of Eternal Truth You may be wondering, “What do I have to *do* to receive this gift from God?” Well, actually, nothing. It is a gift from the Giver. It is simply a fact. Just let it in. You *are* His child. You are beloved of Him. Do not complicate it. Do not block the receiving of this gift with thoughts that you are somehow undeserving. The reality is none of us is “deserving”—all the Father’s gifts are received only through the merits, mercy, and grace of the Holy Messiah,[13](#fn-13) but how His generous heart longs for each of His children to receive them! Then, as your new or renewed understanding dawns on you, rejoice in thanking the Giver of this gift. Since finding my faith in my mid-20s, I have awakened to the realisation that I truly am a son of God. The more fully I internalise this gift, the more keenly I know who I am and how completely I am loved. Some things that have helped me grow in understanding are the words of scripture, my patriarchal blessing, temple worship, serving others, expressing gratitude, and sacred prayer with my Father. I marvel at how earlier sorrow, pain, and grief in my life would have been reframed, soothed, and much of it overcome had I known this beautiful truth. ## Come unto Christ and Receive Moroni exhorts us to “deny not the gifts of God” but to “come unto Christ, and lay hold upon every good gift.”[14](#fn-14) You can come to Christ with confidence in His loving-kindness and receive all His gifts of joy, peace, hope, light, truth, revelation, knowledge, and wisdom—with your head held high, your arms outstretched, and your hands open, ready to receive. And you can receive these gifts because you are secure and grounded in the knowledge that you are a beloved daughter of God, you are a cherished son of God, and He has gifted you His perfect, holy Son to redeem you, justify you, and sanctify you.[15](#fn-15) You *are* a child of God. This is not just a nice song we sing.[16](#fn-16) Will you please accept, open, and receive this gift of knowledge and understanding from Him? Will you hold it close as the precious treasure it is? Re-receive this gift, or perhaps truly receive it for the very first time, and let it transform every aspect of your life. This is the more excellent way God has prepared for you through the gift of His Son.[17](#fn-17) You really are that you might have joy\![18](#fn-18) In the name of Jesus Christ, amen. --- ## Footnotes **1.** See [Isaiah 43:18–20.](../../../scriptures/ot/isa/43.md) **2.** [James 1:17.](../../../scriptures/nt/james/1.md) **3.** See [John 3:16.](../../../scriptures/nt/john/3.md) **4.** [Ether 12:11.](../../../scriptures/bofm/ether/12.md) **5.** See [Romans 8:16](../../../scriptures/nt/rom/8.md); [1 Nephi 11:17](../../../scriptures/bofm/1-ne/11.md). **6.** See [John 3:16–17;](../../../scriptures/nt/john/3.md) [1 Nephi 17:36](../../../scriptures/bofm/1-ne/17.md); [3 Nephi 14:9–11.](../../../scriptures/bofm/3-ne/14.md) **7.** See [Jeremiah 1:5](../../../scriptures/ot/jer/1.md). **8.** See [Romans 6:23](../../../scriptures/nt/rom/6.md); [1 John 5:7, 11, 14](../../../scriptures/nt/1-jn/5.md); [3 Nephi 9:14](../../../scriptures/bofm/3-ne/9.md); [Doctrine and Covenants 14:7](../../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/14.md); [66:12](../../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/66.md). **9.** [Doctrine and Covenants 88:33](../../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/88.md). **10.** See [Psalm 82:6](../../../scriptures/ot/ps/82.md). **11.** See Russell M. Nelson, “[Joy and Spiritual Survival](../../2016/10/joy-and-spiritual-survival.md),” Ensign or Liahona, Nov. 2016, 82. **12.** “The way you think about who you really are affects almost every decision you will ever make” (Russell M. Nelson, “[Choices for Eternity](../../../broadcasts/worldwide-devotional-for-young-adults/2022/05/12nelson.md)” \[worldwide devotional for young adults, May 15, 2022], Gospel Library). **13.** See [2 Nephi 2:8](../../../scriptures/bofm/2-ne/2.md). **14.** [Moroni 10:8, 30.](../../../scriptures/bofm/moro/10.md) **15.** See [Moses 6:57–62.](../../../scriptures/pgp/moses/6.md) **16.** See “I Am a Child of God,” Hymns, no. 301. **17.** See [Ether 12:11.](../../../scriptures/bofm/ether/12.md) **18.** See [2 Nephi 2:25.](../../../scriptures/bofm/2-ne/2.md)
